parsed.message:
Your geyser appears to have a corroded internal tank. This is indicated by the rusty brown water visible in the drip tray and your experience of higher electricity bills and a reduced supply of hot water.

Geyser tanks corrode when the internal sacrificial anode, designed to protect the tank lining, has been fully consumed. Once the anode is gone, the tank's steel wall begins to rust, leading to leaks and compromising the tank's ability to retain heat efficiently.

If left unaddressed, the corrosion will worsen, leading to a more significant leak or a complete burst of the geyser tank. This could cause extensive water damage to your property, including ceilings, walls, and flooring.

There is a risk of electrical shorting if water comes into contact with the geyser's electrical components. It is advisable to switch off the geyser at your electrical distribution board to prevent this hazard.

parsed.message:
Your garage door appears to have a missing counterbalance spring on one side, which is causing the door to hang unevenly. This type of spring system is crucial for balancing the door's weight, allowing the motor to lift it smoothly and safely.

Counterbalance springs, whether torsion or tension, are designed to assist the motor by offsetting the door's heavy load. When one spring fails or goes missing, the entire weight of the door is no longer evenly distributed, leading to the visible tilting and operational issues you are experiencing. This often happens due to metal fatigue over many years of use.

Operating the door in this unbalanced state can cause significant damage to the garage door opener motor, the tracks, and other structural components. The motor will be forced to work much harder than intended, potentially leading to its premature failure. It also creates a safety hazard, as the door could fall unexpectedly.

parsed.message:
Your garage door appears to have a snapped lifting cable on the left side. This cable is crucial for counterbalancing the door's weight and guiding it smoothly along its tracks. When it breaks, the door loses support on that side, causing it to drop, become misaligned, and potentially jam.

Lifting cables typically snap due to wear and tear, corrosion, or repeated stress from a misaligned track or an unbalanced door. Once one cable fails, the entire weight shifts, placing immense strain on the remaining components, including the motor and the other cable.

If left unaddressed, the door's misalignment can worsen, potentially bending the tracks, damaging rollers, or causing further strain on the garage door opener motor. This can lead to more extensive and costly repairs.

Attempting to operate a garage door with a snapped cable can be dangerous. The door is extremely heavy and can fall unexpectedly, causing serious injury or further damage to property.

Attempting to operate or manually lift the door with a broken spring can be dangerous due to the door's unexpected weight and imbalance. It is best to avoid any manual intervention until a professional can assess and repair the issue.

parsed.message:
A missing counterbalance tension spring on the left side of your tilt-up garage door is causing this issue. Tilt-up wooden doors are extremely heavy and rely entirely on a matched pair of high-tension springs to offset their weight so the motor can lift them easily.

When one spring is missing or snaps, the door becomes severely unbalanced and twice as heavy for the opener to lift. As soon as the motor detects this excessive resistance, its built-in safety system interprets the load as an obstruction, stops operation, beeps to alert you, and reverses the door to prevent damage to the motor gears.

Operating the door in this unbalanced state can quickly burn out the opener's motor or strip its drive gears. Additionally, a heavy wooden door without proper spring counterbalance poses a serious safety hazard if it falls unexpectedly.

To help us match you with the right specialist, could you confirm if the missing spring snapped recently, or if you also need the hinge hardware replaced?

parsed.message:
The standing rusty water in your drip tray, combined with a sudden drop in hot water duration and a rising electricity bill, strongly suggests that your geyser's inner cylinder has corroded and breached, or the heating element flange is leaking. When the inner steel tank rusts through, water escapes into the outer casing and pools in the drip tray, carrying rust with it.

This typically develops over several years as the sacrificial anode rod inside the tank degrades. Once the anode is completely depleted, the water begins attacking the steel tank itself, leading to rust, structural failure of the cylinder, and electrical shorting of the element.

If left unresolved, the leak will continue to fill the drip tray, risking an overflow that can cause severe ceiling and structural water damage to your home. Additionally, the compromised heating element will continue to draw excessive power while failing to heat the water efficiently.

parsed.message:
Your garage door is failing to open because the heavy tension spring on the left-hand side is completely missing. These springs act as a counterbalance to offset the immense weight of the wooden door, allowing the motor to lift it with minimal effort. When a spring is absent, the door becomes extremely heavy and unbalanced.

Because the motor is suddenly forced to lift the full deadweight of the timber door, its internal safety sensors detect the extreme resistance as an obstruction. To prevent the motor from burning out, the system automatically stops, beeps an error code, and reverses the door back down. Operating the door in this condition puts severe strain on the opener's drive gear and the remaining right-hand spring.
parsed.action_required: Your technician will source and install a replacement tension spring calibrated to the specific weight of your wooden door. They will then inspect the opposite spring for wear, balance the entire door assembly, and test the motor's force limits to ensure smooth operation.

parsed.message:
The garage door is hanging unevenly because the tension spring on the left side has broken or detached, leaving only the right-side spring intact. Without equal counterbalance on both sides, the door sags on the left, causing the entire assembly to twist and jam when the motor attempts to lift it.

Garage door springs operate under immense tension and fatigue over hundreds of cycles until they eventually snap. When one spring breaks, the motor arm and the remaining hardware are subjected to severe twisting forces they are not designed to handle.

Do not attempt to operate the door using the automatic opener, as the unbalanced weight can easily burn out the motor or bend the central drive rail. Because these springs are under high tension, they are extremely dangerous to adjust or replace without specialised tools.
